module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: The Full Spectrum: Who Draws the Line?
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can state the one question that ties all 21 episodes together.
Subject: Series finale | Run time: about 3 minutes

[WELCOME] Welcome to the last episode of Better Vice Club. This is Anthony McDonald. Three seasons, twenty-one commodities, from the coffee cup in Episode 1 to the khat leaf in Episode 20. Today there is no new substance. Today we hold all of it together and ask what it means.

[HOOK] The honest note one more time. Across this series we have studied substances that range from harmless to deadly, and we have always tried to be honest about both the culture and the harm. This is a history class, not a how-to, and not an endorsement of anything. If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, the SAMHSA helpline is free and confidential, 24 hours: 1-800-662-4357. Now the question that has been building the whole time. Start with two facts. Alcohol and tobacco, two of the most harmful substances humans use, are legal almost everywhere. Psilocybin and cannabis, two of the least harmful, are in the most restrictive legal category in the United States. If harm does not explain the line between legal and criminal, what does? That is the whole finale: who draws the line, and how?

[TEACH] We answer with the same four lenses, now pointed at all 21 episodes at once. Geography lays out the full prohibition map and a peer-reviewed harm ranking that does not match the law (Nutt et al., 2010). Social studies finds the pattern underneath: substance control has repeatedly been population control. Economics confirms one shape across every episode, the people who do the physical work capture the smallest share. And English language arts reads the literature of consciousness across three seasons, and asks who gets called an author and who gets called a drug user. Each lesson is short and ends with a quick question.

[REMEMBER] Hold the thesis of the whole series. The line between a legal substance and a criminal one is drawn by political and economic power, not by pharmacological evidence. That is not an opinion offered at the end. It is the conclusion the evidence across 21 episodes keeps pointing to (Nutt et al., 2010; Alexander, 2010).

[CHECK] Quick check. According to this series, what draws the line between legal and criminal substances? [Beat] Political and economic power, not pharmacological harm (Nutt et al., 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- This finale synthesizes all 21 episodes; it is new understanding, not new information.
- This is education, not a how-to or an endorsement. Help: SAMHSA 1-800-662-4357.
- The two most harmful common substances, alcohol and tobacco, are legal; two of the least harmful, psilocybin and cannabis, are Schedule I (Nutt et al., 2010).
- The series thesis: power, not pharmacology, draws the legal line (Nutt et al., 2010; Alexander, 2010).

## Sources
- Nutt, D. J., King, L. A., & Phillips, L. D. (2010). Drug harms in the UK: A multicriteria decision analysis. The Lancet, 376(9752), 1558-1565.
- Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow: Mass incarceration in the age of colorblindness. The New Press.

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: The Prohibition Map
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can read the full map of all 21 substances and see what legal status tracks.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we opened the finale. Two quick questions.
One: what does this series say draws the legal line? [Beat] Political and economic power, not pharmacology (Nutt et al., 2010).
Two: name two harmful substances that are legal and two mild ones that are not. [Beat] Alcohol and tobacco are legal; psilocybin and cannabis are Schedule I (Nutt et al., 2010).

[HOOK] Lay all 21 commodities on one world map, colored by whether they are legal or criminal, and a pattern jumps out that has nothing to do with how dangerous they are.

[TEACH] Look at what is legal everywhere: coffee, tea, chocolate, sugar, beer, wine, whiskey, and tobacco. Now look at what is criminalized or tightly restricted: cannabis, the coca leaf, psilocybin, and khat. The first group is not safer than the second. Tobacco alone kills about 8 million people a year, and alcohol is the single most harmful substance on a peer-reviewed ranking, while psilocybin sits near the bottom for harm (Nutt et al., 2010). So danger is not the sorting rule. Here is the rule that actually fits. The legal group is the set of substances that became central to European colonial trade. Coffee, sugar, tobacco, and tea built empires, ports, and fortunes, which made them politically impossible to ban. The criminalized group is the set whose main cultural home was Indigenous, non-Western, or tied to communities that Western political power treated as a threat: cannabis with Black and Mexican communities, coca with Andean tradition, psilocybin with the counterculture, khat with the East African diaspora. The map is not a map of harm. It is a map of power and history.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Picture green pins on the colonial commodities, coffee in Ethiopia, tobacco in Virginia, alcohol across Europe, all legal. Now picture red pins on the sacred and non-Western plants, coca in the Andes, psilocybin in Oaxaca, khat in the Horn of Africa, all criminalized. The colors do not follow the danger. They follow who held power when the laws were written.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: on the full map of all 21 substances, legal status tracks colonial trade history and political power, not pharmacological harm, so the colonial commodities are legal everywhere while the sacred and non-Western plants are criminalized (Nutt et al., 2010). The map is about power, not danger.

[CHECK] Quick check. What do the legal substances on the map have in common that the criminalized ones do not? [Beat] They became central to European colonial trade, which made them politically impossible to ban (Nutt et al., 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- All the colonial trade commodities (coffee, tea, sugar, tobacco, alcohol) are legal everywhere.
- The criminalized substances (cannabis, coca, psilocybin, khat) were sacred or non-Western, tied to marginalized communities.
- Harm does not explain the split; tobacco and alcohol are among the most harmful yet legal (Nutt et al., 2010).
- The prohibition map tracks colonial history and political power, not pharmacology.

## Sources
- Nutt, D. J., King, L. A., & Phillips, L. D. (2010). Drug harms in the UK: A multicriteria decision analysis. The Lancet, 376(9752), 1558-1565.

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: Three Patterns in the Map
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can name the three rules that decide which substances are legal.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we read the full prohibition map. Two quick questions.
One: does legal status track harm or power? [Beat] Power and colonial history, not harm (Nutt et al., 2010).
Two: name two legal colonial commodities and two criminalized sacred plants. [Beat] Coffee and tobacco are legal; coca and psilocybin are not.

[HOOK] Once you see that the map is about power, three clear rules appear, and the third one is the sharpest, because it shows the chemistry never mattered at all.

[TEACH] Here are the three patterns. Pattern one: colonial trade equals legal. Any substance that became embedded in European colonial trade, coffee, tea, chocolate, sugar, alcohol, tobacco, is legal everywhere, because its economic infrastructure made a ban unthinkable. Pattern two: sacred or non-Western equals criminalized. Substances whose main cultural context was Indigenous or non-Western, or tied to communities seen as a political threat, became criminal, and the pharmacology does not explain why they are treated differently from tobacco or alcohol. Pattern three is the clincher: legal status changes when the community association changes, even though the chemistry does not. Opium was a legal pharmacy product in the 1800s, sold as laudanum in every American drugstore, until its use was associated with Chinese immigrants, and the Harrison Narcotics Act criminalized it in 1914 (Alexander, 2010). Cannabis was a legal medicine until Harry Anslinger rebranded it as marihuana and tied it to Black and Mexican communities in 1937. The plant did not change. The community attached to it did, and the law followed the community, not the molecule.

[PICTURE] Picture the same bottle of laudanum on a pharmacy shelf in 1900, perfectly legal, and then picture it becoming a crime, not because anything inside the bottle changed, but because of who the public pictured using it. Hold that image. It is the whole argument in one bottle.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: three patterns sort the map, colonial trade equals legal, sacred or non-Western equals criminalized, and legal status changes when the community association changes while the chemistry stays the same, as with opium in 1914 and cannabis in 1937 (Alexander, 2010). The law follows the community, not the molecule.

[CHECK] Quick check. What changed when opium and cannabis went from legal to criminal, if not their chemistry? [Beat] The community associated with them changed, and the law followed that association (Alexander, 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- Pattern 1: substances embedded in colonial trade are legal everywhere.
- Pattern 2: sacred or non-Western substances, tied to marginalized communities, were criminalized.
- Pattern 3: legal status changes when the community association changes, not when the chemistry does.
- Opium (Harrison Act, 1914) and cannabis (1937) show the law following the community, not the molecule (Alexander, 2010).

## Sources
- Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow: Mass incarceration in the age of colorblindness. The New Press.

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: Power, Not Pharmacology
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can use the Nutt harm ranking to show that law does not track danger.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we named the three patterns. Two quick questions.
One: what does Pattern 3 say changes a substance's legal status? [Beat] The community associated with it, not its chemistry (Alexander, 2010).
Two: which pattern makes colonial commodities legal everywhere? [Beat] Pattern 1, colonial trade equals legal.

[HOOK] In 2010 a team of scientists did something simple and devastating. They ranked common substances by how much harm they cause, then laid that ranking next to the law. The two lists barely match.

[TEACH] The study is Nutt and colleagues, published in The Lancet, and it scores each substance from 0 to 100 by combining harm to the user and harm to others (Nutt et al., 2010). Here is what they found. The single most harmful substance is alcohol, scored at 72, and it is legal. Tobacco scores 26, also legal, and it kills about 8 million people a year worldwide. Now look at the bottom of the harm list. LSD scores 7. Psilocybin mushrooms score 6. These are among the least harmful substances measured, and both are Schedule I, the most restrictive category in United States law, the same category as heroin. Sit with the core observation. The two substances ranked most harmful are legal everywhere, and six of the eight ranked least harmful are Schedule I. If the law were built on pharmacological evidence, the ranking and the legal status would line up. They do not. That gap is the proof, in one peer-reviewed table, of the whole series thesis: the line is drawn by power, not by pharmacology.

[PICTURE] Picture two columns. On the left, substances ranked from most harmful at the top to least harmful at the bottom. On the right, their legal status. Draw a line connecting each substance to its law. The lines cross wildly, the most harmful sitting at legal, the least harmful sitting at Schedule I. That tangle of crossing lines is the argument.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: in the peer-reviewed Nutt ranking, alcohol is the most harmful substance and tobacco is high on the list, both legal, while psilocybin and LSD are among the least harmful, both Schedule I, so legal status does not track harm (Nutt et al., 2010). The evidence and the law do not match.

[CHECK] Quick check. In the Nutt ranking, what is the most harmful substance, and is it legal? [Beat] Alcohol, scored 72, the most harmful, and it is legal almost everywhere (Nutt et al., 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- Nutt and colleagues (2010) scored substances 0 to 100 for total harm, peer-reviewed in The Lancet.
- Alcohol ranks most harmful (72) and tobacco ranks high (26); both are legal.
- Psilocybin (6) and LSD (7) are among the least harmful, yet both are Schedule I.
- Six of the eight least harmful substances are Schedule I, so law does not track harm (Nutt et al., 2010).

## Sources
- Nutt, D. J., King, L. A., & Phillips, L. D. (2010). Drug harms in the UK: A multicriteria decision analysis. The Lancet, 376(9752), 1558-1565.

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: The Social Control Pattern
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how substance control has repeatedly been population control.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we read the Nutt harm ranking next to the law. Two quick questions.
One: what is the most harmful substance in the Nutt ranking, and is it legal? [Beat] Alcohol, scored 72, the most harmful, and it is legal almost everywhere (Nutt et al., 2010).
Two: how many of the eight least harmful substances are Schedule I? [Beat] Six of the eight, the most restrictive category, even though the evidence ranks them among the least harmful (Nutt et al., 2010).

[HOOK] Once you accept that the line is drawn by power, a harder question follows. Power over what? Look across all 21 episodes and the answer is always the same. Power over people.

[TEACH] Here is the pattern that runs through every season of this series. In case after case, controlling a substance was a way to control a population that those in power saw as a threat, and the substance itself was not the real concern. Watch it repeat. In the 17th century, governments shut down coffeehouses, not because coffee was dangerous, but because coffeehouses were where people gathered to discuss politics and organize dissent. Charles II issued a proclamation against English coffeehouses in 1675, and the Ottoman Empire banned them too. Colonial missionaries in the Pacific framed kava as a primitive ritual, because the kava ceremony carried Indigenous governance, and undercutting the ceremony undercut the authority that came with it. American Prohibition, from 1920 to 1933, lined up with nativist politics against Irish, German, and Italian immigrant communities, and the ban on alcohol was substantially a ban on those communities' social institutions. Then comes the part Season 3 documented most plainly. Cannabis criminalization was aimed at Black communities and the antiwar movement, and John Ehrlichman, Nixon's own domestic policy advisor, gave a documented admission that the drug war was built to target those communities through pharmacological pretext, an admission recovered and reported by Dan Baum (Baum, 2016). Opioids and pharmaceutical marketing targeted economically displaced communities. The 100 to 1 crack versus powder cocaine sentencing disparity fell on Black urban communities, encoding racial inequality in formally neutral legal language (Alexander, 2010). Psychedelics were tied to the counterculture and criminalized alongside it. And the United Kingdom banned khat over the advice of its own scientific advisory council, with the ban falling on the East African diaspora. Eight cases, three seasons, one mechanism.

[PICTURE] Picture a single hand reaching for very different things across four centuries. A coffeehouse door in London. A kava bowl in the Pacific. A tavern in immigrant Chicago. A leaf in an Indianapolis afternoon. The hand is not reaching for the substance. It is reaching for the people gathered around it. Hold that image, because it is the whole pattern in one gesture.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: across all three seasons, substance control has functioned as a proxy for population control, banning the substance to reach the community gathered around it, from coffeehouses in 1675 to the crack versus powder disparity to the UK khat ban (Baum, 2016; Alexander, 2010). The substance was rarely the real concern.

[CHECK] Quick check. What did substance control most often actually control, and name one documented example. [Beat] It controlled populations seen as a political threat, not the substance itself; for example, the cannabis drug war aimed at Black and antiwar communities, per Ehrlichman's documented admission reported by Baum (Baum, 2016).

## Key Takeaways
- Across 21 episodes, controlling a substance was repeatedly a way to control a population, not the chemical.
- Coffeehouse bans (1675), kava as "primitive ritual," and alcohol Prohibition all targeted communities, not the drink.
- Ehrlichman's documented admission, reported by Baum, ties the cannabis drug war to Black and antiwar communities (Baum, 2016).
- The 100 to 1 crack versus powder disparity and the UK khat ban continued the same pattern (Alexander, 2010).

## Sources
- Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow: Mass incarceration in the age of colorblindness. The New Press.
- Baum, D. (2016). Legalize it all. Harper's Magazine, April 2016.

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: The Eight Patterns
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can name the eight repeating patterns the series found across 21 episodes.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we traced the social control pattern. Two quick questions.
One: what did substance control most often actually control? [Beat] A population seen as a political threat, not the substance itself.
Two: name one documented example. [Beat] The cannabis drug war aimed at Black and antiwar communities, per Ehrlichman's admission reported by Baum (Baum, 2016).

[HOOK] Twenty-one episodes is a lot of detail. But detail is not understanding. Understanding is when the detail collapses into a small number of rules that keep coming true. The series found eight, and once you have them, you can hold every episode at once.

[TEACH] Here are the eight patterns. The first four were named across Seasons 1 and 2. Pattern one, Geography Insists: the ground speaks first, and soil, elevation, and climate shape the plant before any human decision. Pattern two, It Begins Sacred: nearly every substance had a sacred or ceremonial origin before it became a commercial product. Pattern three, Labor Captures the Smallest Share: the smile curve holds, value collects at the branded ends of the supply chain while the farmer in the middle earns the least. Pattern four, Narrative Control: whoever controls the story controls the meaning, deciding which use is called tradition and which is called abuse. Then Season 3 added four more that extend across all three seasons. Pattern five, Power Draws the Line: legal status is set by political and economic power, not by pharmacology, and the Nutt harm ranking laid next to the law is the evidence (Nutt et al., 2010). Pattern six, Sacred Becomes Criminal: sacred Indigenous substances are disproportionately criminalized when they are tied to marginalized communities, from coca to peyote to khat. Pattern seven, Extraction Follows Extraction: the same communities bear the costs across centuries, from colonial commodity extraction to corporate extraction to pharmaceutical and criminal extraction. Pattern eight, Criminalization Transfers Profit: banning a substance does not erase its market, it hands that market to criminal organizations, the way Prohibition built organized crime and cannabis prohibition handed billions to black markets.

[PICTURE] Picture a wall with eight cards pinned in two rows. The top row, the original four, reads Geography, Sacred, Labor, Narrative. The bottom row, the four Season 3 added, reads Power, Sacred to Criminal, Extraction, Profit Transfer. Every one of the 21 episodes is a thread you could run from a substance up to one of these eight cards. Hold the wall in your mind. That is the series in eight cards.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the series found eight repeating patterns, four from Seasons 1 and 2 (Geography Insists, It Begins Sacred, Labor Captures the Smallest Share, Narrative Control) and four added in Season 3 (Power Draws the Line, Sacred Becomes Criminal, Extraction Follows Extraction, Criminalization Transfers Profit), with the Nutt ranking standing as the evidence for Pattern five (Nutt et al., 2010).

[CHECK] Quick check. What does Pattern five say, and what is its evidence? [Beat] Power Draws the Line: legal status is set by political and economic power, not pharmacology, and the Nutt harm ranking next to the law is the evidence (Nutt et al., 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- The first four patterns, from Seasons 1 and 2: Geography Insists, It Begins Sacred, Labor Captures the Smallest Share, Narrative Control.
- Season 3 added four: Power Draws the Line, Sacred Becomes Criminal, Extraction Follows Extraction, Criminalization Transfers Profit.
- Pattern five rests on the Nutt harm ranking laid next to legal status (Nutt et al., 2010).
- All 21 episodes can be traced to one or more of these eight patterns, which is how detail becomes understanding.

## Sources
- Nutt, D. J., King, L. A., & Phillips, L. D. (2010). Drug harms in the UK: A multicriteria decision analysis. The Lancet, 376(9752), 1558-1565.

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: Structural, Not Just Intentional
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain structural racism as distinct from intentional racism, using the series evidence.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we named the eight patterns. Two quick questions.
One: what does Pattern five say? [Beat] Power Draws the Line: legal status is set by political and economic power, not pharmacology (Nutt et al., 2010).
Two: what does Pattern seven say? [Beat] Extraction Follows Extraction: the same communities bear the costs across centuries, from colonial to corporate to pharmaceutical and criminal extraction.

[HOOK] When people hear that drug policy produced racial harm, they often jump to one question. Did someone mean it? That question feels like the important one. It is not. There is a sharper question, and Michelle Alexander built a whole framework around it.

[TEACH] In The New Jim Crow, Michelle Alexander makes an argument that takes a moment to settle in. A system can produce deeply unequal racial outcomes even when not every actor intends racism, because the structure itself does the channeling (Alexander, 2010). Think about what a structure decides. Who gets policed. Who gets charged. Who gets sentenced, and for how long. None of those steps requires a single person to hold a racist belief for the result to fall heaviest on one community. The harm is built into the machinery, not only into the hearts of the people running it. Now connect this to the series. Pattern seven told us the same communities appear again and again as the ones bearing costs, across colonial, corporate, and pharmaceutical extraction. That is not a coincidence you would expect from random individual choices. It is what a structure produces. The clearest single mechanism is the 100 to 1 crack versus powder cocaine sentencing disparity. The two substances are chemically close. The sentences were not. Written in formally neutral language, the disparity sent crack cases, concentrated in Black urban communities, into far longer prison terms than powder cases (Alexander, 2010). No clause in that law named a race. The structure did the work the language did not say out loud. So here is the point for you to carry. Asking whether a policy was meant to be racist is a smaller question than asking whether it reliably produces racial harm. The first question is about intent, which is hard to prove and easy to deny. The second is about outcomes, which can be measured. And the series evidence answers the second question across three seasons. The same communities, the same costs, the same machinery, over and over. This is documented analysis, not a verdict on anyone's heart.

[PICTURE] Picture two doors leading into the same building. One door is labeled "Did they mean it?" The other is labeled "Does it reliably hurt the same people?" Most arguments crowd at the first door and never get inside. Alexander's framework walks you through the second door, where the evidence actually lives. Hold the image of the second door. That is where the series has been standing the whole time.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Alexander's framework shows a system can produce unequal racial outcomes without every actor intending racism, because the structure decides who is policed, charged, and sentenced; the crack versus powder disparity is that structure made visible, and the series evidence answers the outcome question across three seasons (Alexander, 2010).

[CHECK] Quick check. Which question does Alexander's framework treat as the more important one, intent or outcome, and why? [Beat] Outcome, whether a policy reliably produces racial harm, because a structure can channel that harm even without every actor intending it, and outcomes can be measured (Alexander, 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- Structural racism produces unequal outcomes even without every actor intending racism (Alexander, 2010).
- The structure decides who is policed, charged, and sentenced, and that channels the harm.
- The 100 to 1 crack versus powder disparity is a structural mechanism, not a matter of individual intent (Alexander, 2010).
- The series asks the outcome question, does a policy reliably produce racial harm, and answers it across three seasons.

## Sources
- Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow: Mass incarceration in the age of colorblindness. The New Press.

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: The Full Cost
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can compare market size, harm, and who pays across all three seasons.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we read "Structural, Not Just Intentional." Two quick questions.
One: what is the difference between structural racism and intentional racism? [Beat] Structural racism is built into the systems and rules, so it produces unequal outcomes even when no single person intends harm, while intentional racism is a conscious individual choice.
Two: which communities keep recurring as the cost-bearers across the seasons? [Beat] The same producing and policed communities every time, coffee and cacao farmers in Season 1, vineyard and rickhouse workers in Season 2, and Appalachian, Black urban, and East African diaspora communities in Season 3.

[HOOK] Now let us put a number on it. When you line up all 21 substances by market size next to the harm they cause, the picture is not just unequal. It is upside down.

[TEACH] Here is the full cost accounting across three seasons. Alcohol is the largest market we studied, about one and a half trillion dollars a year, and it causes about three million deaths a year (World Health Organization, 2024). It is legal almost everywhere. Tobacco is about an eight hundred billion dollar market, and it causes about eight million deaths a year (World Health Organization, 2024). It is legal everywhere, with no outright bans. Now set those beside two substances from Season 1 and Season 3. Coffee is about a four hundred fifty billion dollar market with roughly zero direct deaths, yet its farmers keep only two to six percent of what the cup sells for. Khat is a far smaller market, roughly three hundred fifty to five hundred million dollars in Ethiopian exports, with roughly zero direct deaths. And khat is the one that got banned abroad, in the United Kingdom, the United States, Canada, and most of Western Europe. Sit with the sharpest comparison in the series. Alcohol, legal, causes about three million deaths a year. Khat, banned in the UK and the US, causes roughly zero (World Health Organization, 2024; Nutt et al., 2010). If policy tracked harm, those two would trade places. They do not. Policy is following economic and political power, not the body count.

[PICTURE] Picture two stacks of money on a table. The alcohol stack is enormous, one and a half trillion dollars, and beside it sits a tall column of three million names. The khat stack is tiny, a few hundred million dollars, and beside it sits no column at all. Now picture the law walking in. It puts handcuffs on the tiny stack with no names, and it pours the giant stack a drink. That backwards gesture is the whole lesson in one image.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: alcohol is about a one and a half trillion dollar market causing about three million deaths a year, tobacco is about eight hundred billion dollars causing about eight million deaths a year, both legal, while khat is a few hundred million dollar market causing roughly zero deaths and banned in the West (World Health Organization, 2024; Nutt et al., 2010). The harm and the law do not match, which means something other than harm is drawing the line.

[CHECK] Quick check. Which substance has the larger market and the higher death toll, alcohol or khat, and which one is banned in the United Kingdom and the United States? [Beat] Alcohol has the far larger market and the far higher death toll, about three million a year, yet khat, which causes roughly zero, is the one banned in the UK and the US (World Health Organization, 2024).

## Key Takeaways
- Alcohol is about a one and a half trillion dollar market causing about three million deaths a year, and it is legal almost everywhere (World Health Organization, 2024).
- Tobacco is about an eight hundred billion dollar market causing about eight million deaths a year, with no country banning it (World Health Organization, 2024).
- Coffee, about four hundred fifty billion dollars, causes roughly zero direct deaths, yet its farmers keep only two to six percent of retail.
- Khat, roughly three hundred fifty to five hundred million dollars in exports, causes roughly zero deaths, yet it is the one banned abroad, showing policy follows power, not harm (Nutt et al., 2010).

## Sources
- World Health Organization. (2024). Global status report on alcohol and health. WHO Press.
- World Health Organization. (2024). Tobacco fact sheet.
- Nutt, D. J., King, L. A., & Phillips, L. D. (2010). Drug harms in the UK: A multicriteria decision analysis. The Lancet, 376(9752), 1558-1565.

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: The Smile Curve, Confirmed
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain the smile curve and how it held across all 21 episodes.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we did the full cost accounting. Two quick questions.
One: roughly how many deaths does alcohol cause a year? [Beat] About three million a year, in a market worth about one and a half trillion dollars (World Health Organization, 2024).
Two: how much of the retail price does the coffee farmer keep? [Beat] Only about two to six percent of what the cup sells for.

[HOOK] There is one shape that explains where all that money goes. We named it back in Season 2, and across 21 episodes it never broke. Once. Picture a smile.

[TEACH] The shape is called the smile curve, from the economist Mudambi (2008). The idea is simple. If you draw a supply chain from left to right, design and branding on one end, the physical work in the middle, marketing and retail on the other end, and you plot how much money each step keeps, the line curves like a smile. It is high at the two corners and low in the center. The two ends, the design and the branding on one side and the marketing and the retail on the other, capture the most value. The physical middle, the farming, the production, the manufacturing, captures the least. The people doing the hardest physical labor sit at the bottom center of the smile, where the curve dips lowest. The series confirmed this across all 21 episodes. Coffee, tea, chocolate, and sugar in Season 1. Wine, bourbon, tequila, and sake in Season 2. And then the criminalized markets of Season 3, where the curve still holds, except criminal organizations replace the multinational corporations at the profitable ends. The cartel that controls branding and distribution captures the largest share, while the coca farmer or the poppy harvester still captures the smallest. Same shape, different owners at the corners.

[PICTURE] Picture the smile drawn across a chalkboard. At the left corner, high up, a designer sketching a label. At the right corner, high up, a billboard and a checkout counter. And down at the very bottom, in the dip, a single farmer bent over a plant in the field. Everyone at the corners is standing tall. The one person doing the growing is at the lowest point of the curve. That dip is where the labor lives.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the smile curve (Mudambi, 2008) shows value capture is highest at the two ends, design and branding and marketing and retail, and lowest in the physical middle of farming and production, and it held across all 21 episodes, with criminal organizations simply taking the corporations' place at the corners in Season 3. There was one exception. Khat. Its 48-hour freshness requirement blocks multinational consolidation and keeps the supply chain short, so the farmers keep more. That is geography acting as economic protection.

[CHECK] Quick check. Across all 21 episodes, what was the single exception to the smile curve, and why? [Beat] Khat, because its 48-hour freshness requirement blocks multinational consolidation and keeps the supply chain short, so producers keep more, an example of geography as economic protection (Mudambi, 2008).

## Key Takeaways
- The smile curve (Mudambi, 2008) shows value capture is highest at the two ends, design and branding and marketing and retail, and lowest in the physical middle.
- The physical labor, the farming and production, sits at the bottom center where the curve dips lowest.
- The curve held across all 21 episodes; in Season 3 criminal organizations simply replace corporations at the profitable corners.
- The one exception is khat, where a 48-hour freshness requirement blocks consolidation and keeps the chain short, an example of geography as economic protection.

## Sources
- Mudambi, R. (2008). Location, control and innovation in knowledge-intensive industries. Journal of Economic Geography, 8(5), 699-725.

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: Criminalization Transfers Profit
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how banning a substance hands its market to criminal organizations.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we drew the smile curve. Two quick questions.
One: where does value concentrate on the smile curve? [Beat] At the two ends, design and branding on one side and marketing and retail on the other, while the physical middle keeps the least (Mudambi, 2008).
Two: what was the one exception across all 21 episodes? [Beat] Khat, because its 48-hour freshness requirement blocks multinational consolidation and keeps the supply chain short.

[HOOK] Here is the part people get backwards. They think banning a substance makes the money disappear. It does not. It just changes whose hands the money lands in. And the new hands are worse.

[TEACH] Across the series, criminalized markets followed one economic formula. Step one, criminalization removes the legal supply. Step two, with legal supply gone but demand unchanged, the price climbs into a premium. Step three, that premium transfers to whoever controls the illegal market, which means the criminal organizations. The series is full of examples. Alcohol Prohibition in the 1920s did not end drinking. It handed the alcohol trade to organized crime and built the fortunes that made those gangs powerful. Cannabis prohibition pushed an enormous market underground for decades. The eventual legal cannabis market in the United States is worth about forty-seven billion dollars, and for most of the last century all of that value flowed through black markets instead of taxed, regulated businesses. The cocaine market, more than one hundred billion dollars, was captured by cartels precisely because criminalization made a legal supply impossible. No legal company can sell it, so only outlaws can, and the whole premium flows to them. Notice what does not change through any of this. The person at the bottom of the smile curve, the farmer growing the plant, still keeps the smallest share whether the corners are run by corporations or by cartels (Mudambi, 2008).

[PICTURE] Picture a river of money flowing past a customs gate marked legal, taxed, regulated. Now slam the gate shut. The river does not dry up. It just cuts a new channel around the gate, through the woods, into hands that pay no tax, follow no rule, and answer to no court. Same water, same volume, worse destination. That rerouted river is what a ban actually does to the money.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: prohibition does not remove the money from a substance, it moves the money. It takes the value out of legal, taxed, regulated hands and hands it to criminal organizations, seen in alcohol Prohibition in the 1920s, in the cannabis market worth about forty-seven billion dollars in the US, and in the cocaine market worth more than one hundred billion dollars captured by cartels. And through all of it, the physical-labor share at the bottom of the curve stays the smallest (Mudambi, 2008).

[CHECK] Quick check. When a substance is banned, what happens to the money its market generates? [Beat] It does not disappear. The ban removes legal supply, which creates a price premium, and that premium transfers from legal, taxed, regulated businesses to the criminal organizations that control the illegal market.

## Key Takeaways
- The formula across the series: criminalization removes legal supply, creates a price premium, and transfers that premium to criminal organizations.
- Alcohol Prohibition in the 1920s handed the alcohol trade to organized crime.
- Cannabis prohibition pushed an eventual forty-seven billion dollar US market into black markets for decades; the cocaine market, more than one hundred billion dollars, was captured by cartels.
- Prohibition moves the money rather than removing it, and the physical-labor share at the bottom of the smile curve stays the smallest (Mudambi, 2008).

## Sources
- Mudambi, R. (2008). Location, control and innovation in knowledge-intensive industries. Journal of Economic Geography, 8(5), 699-725.

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: The Literature of Consciousness
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can survey how each season produced its own kind of writing about these substances.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we looked at criminalization as an economic act. Two quick questions.
One: what does criminalization do to a substance's market? [Beat] It does not remove the market; it transfers the profit to an illegal supply chain and raises the price.
Two: name one example. [Beat] Opium under prohibition, where the same demand kept flowing through illegal channels at a far higher price.

[HOOK] Here is something easy to miss. Across all three seasons, humans never just used these substances. They wrote about them. And the writing itself is evidence of how each era understood what it was holding.

[TEACH] Think of the whole series as a library, with three shelves, one per season.

Season 1 produced sacred texts and origin stories. The Ethiopian origin myth of Kaldi explains where coffee came from and why a daily cup carries meaning. Okakura Kakuzo's The Book of Tea, from 1906, connects a simple beverage to a whole aesthetic and spiritual tradition. Pacific Island kava ceremonial protocols are oral texts that govern diplomacy and decision-making. Every one of these connects a substance to meaning and to origin. The writing answers the question, where did this come from and what does it mean to us.

Season 2 produced the texts of civilization. The Hymn to Ninkasi, from around 1800 BCE, is at once a prayer to the Sumerian beer goddess and a working brewing recipe, the oldest written evidence of human brewing. The Persian wine poetry of Rumi and Hafez uses the glass of wine as a metaphor for the divine encounter. And bourbon heritage marketing constructed a sense of authenticity, a story of tradition wrapped around a product. The writing here answers a different question, how does this substance hold a whole society together.

Season 3 produced the texts of power. Thomas De Quincey's Confessions of an English Opium-Eater, from 1821, is the first great addiction narrative, holding pleasure and destruction in a single voice. Harry Anslinger's 1937 congressional testimony used racial framing as a policy instrument, turning fear into law. Purdue Pharma's marketing told doctors that pain is the fifth vital sign, false authority dressed as medicine. And Aldous Huxley's The Doors of Perception, from 1954, is a philosopher trying to describe what cannot be described. The writing here answers the sharpest question of all, who gets to control the story.

The throughline runs the length of the shelf. Across three seasons, humans kept writing about these substances, and the kind of text each era produced tells you how that era understood them. Sacred substances generated ceremonial texts. Commercial substances generated marketing. Contested substances generated confessions, propaganda, and testimony.

[PICTURE] Picture three bookshelves side by side. On the first, a painted codex and a book of tea ceremony. On the second, a clay tablet that is both hymn and recipe. On the third, a Victorian memoir, a congressional transcript, and a marketing slogan. Same human impulse, to write about consciousness, three very different shelves. The shelf tells you the century.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: each season produced its own kind of writing. Season 1 produced sacred texts and origin stories, like the myth of Kaldi and Okakura's Book of Tea. Season 2 produced the texts of civilization, like the Hymn to Ninkasi and Persian wine poetry. Season 3 produced the texts of power, from De Quincey's Confessions to Anslinger's testimony to Purdue's marketing to Huxley's Doors of Perception. The writing is the evidence.

[CHECK] Quick check. Which season produced the texts of power, and name one of them? [Beat] Season 3, and any of De Quincey's Confessions, Anslinger's 1937 testimony, Purdue Pharma's marketing, or Huxley's Doors of Perception.

## Key Takeaways
- Across three seasons, humans never just used these substances; they wrote about them, and the writing is evidence of how each era understood them.
- Season 1 produced sacred texts and origin stories: the myth of Kaldi, Okakura's Book of Tea, Pacific kava ceremonial protocols.
- Season 2 produced the texts of civilization: the Hymn to Ninkasi (both prayer and recipe), Persian wine poetry, bourbon heritage marketing.
- Season 3 produced the texts of power: De Quincey's Confessions, Anslinger's 1937 testimony, Purdue's "pain is the fifth vital sign," and Huxley's Doors of Perception.

## Sources
- Quinones, S. (2015). Dreamland: The true tale of America's opiate epidemic. Bloomsbury Press.
- Keefe, P. R. (2021). Empire of pain: The secret history of the Sackler dynasty. Doubleday.

(Series literary inventory drawn from the Better Vice Club Episode 21 synthesis, Data Sheet D, "The Literature of Consciousness," which catalogs these texts across all three seasons. The two works above support Season 3's documented texts of power.)

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: Four Patterns in the Literature
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can name the four patterns that run through the writing across all three seasons.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 8 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we surveyed the literature of consciousness, season by season. Two quick questions.
One: name one Season 1 sacred text. [Beat] The Ethiopian myth of Kaldi, Okakura's Book of Tea, or the Pacific kava ceremonial protocols.
Two: name one Season 3 text of power. [Beat] De Quincey's Confessions, Anslinger's 1937 testimony, Purdue's marketing, or Huxley's Doors of Perception.

[HOOK] Once you lay all that writing out on one table, four patterns appear. They are not about any single substance. They are about who controls the story, and how the story survives.

[TEACH] Here are the four patterns.

Pattern A: sacred texts become commodities. The text does not disappear; it gets absorbed into a commercial narrative. The Aztec cacao codices were destroyed by colonizers and later replaced by corporate "heritage" marketing for chocolate. Maria Sabina's ceremonial chants, the velada, were published and circulated worldwide without her community's consent. The sacred document is torn from its origin and sold back as a brand.

Pattern B: propaganda fills the evidence gap. Wherever policy lacked a pharmacological justification, propaganda appeared to fill the hole. The film Reefer Madness supplied fear where no evidence supported cannabis criminalization. Cigarette advertising claimed doctors approved, when the evidence pointed the other way. The "Just Say No" campaign offered a two-word slogan exactly where a structural analysis would have demanded a structural response. Propaganda shows up precisely where evidence is absent.

Pattern C: investigative journalism recovers suppressed narratives. This is the counterweight, but it only works when journalists ask the right questions. Sam Quinones reconstructed the human geography of the opioid crisis, building it from many storylines because the crisis could not be seen from one vantage point (Quinones, 2015). Patrick Radden Keefe traced the Sackler family and the dynasty behind the marketing (Keefe, 2021). Dan Baum surfaced the documented admission behind the war on drugs, an on-the-record account of who it was meant to target (Baum, 2016). Journalism is a cultural tool for restoring what was erased, when someone bothers to dig.

Pattern D: oral traditions are the most durable archive. When institutions fail to preserve, or deliberately erase, communities remember. A whiskey distiller's story was kept in a family's oral tradition for 130 years while the institutional record stayed silent. Mazatec ceremony knowledge survived colonization inside the velada itself. Somali gabay, a tradition of oral poetry, survives in the diaspora inside the afternoon khat session, in a language most of the surrounding city does not speak. The most fragile-looking form, words passed mouth to mouth, turns out to be the toughest archive of all.

Put the four together and you can read any of these texts faster. Sacred texts get commodified. Propaganda fills evidence gaps. Journalism recovers what was buried. And oral tradition outlasts the institutions that tried to erase it.

[PICTURE] Picture a single long table covered in documents. A burned codex and a corporate chocolate ad sit next to each other, that is Pattern A. A propaganda film reel where a stack of studies should be, that is Pattern B. A reporter's notebook reopening a sealed file, that is Pattern C. And an elder speaking, with no paper at all, outlasting every printed thing on the table, that is Pattern D.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: four patterns run through the literature. A, sacred texts become commodities. B, propaganda fills the evidence gap. C, investigative journalism recovers suppressed narratives, as with Quinones (2015), Keefe (2021), and Baum (2016). D, oral traditions are the most durable archive. Together they tell you who controlled the story and how the truth survived anyway.

[CHECK] Quick check. Which pattern says the writing appears exactly where the evidence is missing, and name one example? [Beat] Pattern B, propaganda fills the evidence gap, shown by Reefer Madness, the doctors-approve cigarette ads, or "Just Say No."

## Key Takeaways
- Pattern A: sacred texts become commodities, as with the destroyed Aztec cacao codices replaced by "heritage" marketing, and Maria Sabina's chants published without consent.
- Pattern B: propaganda fills the evidence gap, from Reefer Madness to cigarette ads to "Just Say No."
- Pattern C: investigative journalism recovers suppressed narratives, as with Quinones (2015), Keefe (2021), and Baum (2016), but only when journalists ask the right questions.
- Pattern D: oral traditions are the most durable archive, from a 130-year family whiskey story to Mazatec ceremony to Somali gabay in the diaspora khat session.

## Sources
- Quinones, S. (2015). Dreamland: The true tale of America's opiate epidemic. Bloomsbury Press.
- Keefe, P. R. (2021). Empire of pain: The secret history of the Sackler dynasty. Doubleday.
- Baum, D. (2016). Legalize it all. Harper's Magazine, April 2016.

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: Key Terms: The Full Spectrum
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can define the core vocabulary of the series finale across all four lenses.
Subject: Vocabulary | Run time: about 5 minutes

Your word bank for the finale. Read it quick, out loud if you can. The full glossary lives in the course Resources. If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, the SAMHSA helpline is 1-800-662-4357.

## Geography

**Prohibition map.** A map of all 21 substances by legal status, which tracks colonial history and power rather than harm (Nutt et al., 2010).

**Colonial commodity.** A substance embedded in European colonial trade, which made it politically impossible to ban.

**Harm ranking.** The Nutt and colleagues (2010) score from 0 to 100 combining harm to the user and to others, which does not match legal status.

**Schedule I.** The most restrictive US drug category, defined as having no accepted medical use, holding psilocybin, cannabis, and heroin alike.

## Social Studies

**Social control.** Using control of a substance to control a population perceived as a threat by those in power.

**Structural racism.** A system that reliably produces racial harm even without every actor intending it (Alexander, 2010).

**Narrative control.** Whoever controls the story about a substance controls its meaning and its policy.

**Pattern.** A repeating structure the series found across all 21 episodes, eight in total across three seasons.

## Economics

**Smile curve.** The pattern (Mudambi, 2008) where value is highest at the supply chain's ends and lowest in the physical middle.

**Value capture.** The share of a supply chain's total value that a given participant keeps.

**Price premium.** The extra price created when criminalization removes legal supply, which transfers to criminal organizations.

## English Language Arts

**Texts of power.** Season 3's writing about substances, where policy, propaganda, and addiction narratives meet.

**Propaganda.** Persuasion that fills the gap where policy lacks evidence, like the film Reefer Madness.

**Investigative journalism.** Reporting that recovers suppressed narratives, as Quinones, Keefe, and Baum did.

**Oral tradition.** Knowledge preserved by community memory, often the most durable archive when institutions erase the record.

## Sources
- Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow: Mass incarceration in the age of colorblindness. The New Press.
- Mudambi, R. (2008). Location, control and innovation in knowledge-intensive industries. Journal of Economic Geography, 8(5), 699-725.
- Nutt, D. J., King, L. A., & Phillips, L. D. (2010). Drug harms in the UK: A multicriteria decision analysis. The Lancet, 376(9752), 1558-1565.

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: Who Draws the Line: The Series in One Breath
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can state the series conclusion and the evidence from all four lenses behind it.
Subject: Review | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] This is the last review of Better Vice Club. Let's pull all 21 episodes together, one thread per lens, then the conclusion they all point to.

[TEACH] Geography first. Lay every substance on one map and legal status tracks colonial trade and political power, not harm. The proof is the peer-reviewed Nutt ranking: alcohol is the most harmful substance and it is legal, while psilocybin and LSD are among the least harmful and both are Schedule I (Nutt et al., 2010). Six of the eight least harmful substances are Schedule I. The evidence and the law do not match.

Now social studies. Underneath the map is a pattern of social control: from suppressed coffeehouses to immigrant-targeted Prohibition to a war on drugs aimed at Black communities and the antiwar movement, controlling a substance has repeatedly meant controlling a population (Baum, 2016). The series names eight patterns across three seasons, and Alexander's framework shows the harm is structural, built into who gets policed and sentenced, not just a matter of intent (Alexander, 2010).

Then economics. One shape held across all 21 episodes: the smile curve, where value concentrates at the branding and retail ends while the people doing the physical work capture the least (Mudambi, 2008). Criminalization does not remove the money, it transfers it to criminal organizations, and the only exception to the whole pattern was khat, protected by its 48-hour freshness. Alcohol, legal, causes about 3 million deaths a year; khat, banned, causes roughly zero (World Health Organization, 2024).

Last, language. Across three seasons humans kept writing about these substances, sacred origin texts, the texts of civilization, the texts of power, and four patterns ran through all of it: sacred texts become commodities, propaganda fills the evidence gap, investigative journalism recovers what was erased, and oral traditions outlast the institutions that tried to erase them (Quinones, 2015).

[REMEMBER] Here is the conclusion of the entire series, in one sentence. The line between a legal substance and a criminal one is drawn by political and economic power and colonial history, not by pharmacological evidence, and that is not an opinion offered at the end but the conclusion the evidence across 21 episodes keeps pointing to (Nutt et al., 2010; Alexander, 2010). Who draws the line? Power does. Now you can see it.

[CHECK] One last check before the final quiz. What single conclusion do all 21 episodes point to? [Beat] That power and history, not pharmacology, draw the line between legal and criminal substances (Nutt et al., 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- Geography: the prohibition map tracks colonial power, and the Nutt ranking shows law does not follow harm (Nutt et al., 2010).
- Social studies: substance control has been population control, and the harm is structural (Baum, 2016; Alexander, 2010).
- Economics: the smile curve held across 21 episodes, and criminalization transfers profit to criminal organizations (Mudambi, 2008).
- Language: four patterns run through the literature, and the series concludes power, not pharmacology, draws the line.

## Sources
- Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow. The New Press.
- Baum, D. (2016). Legalize it all. Harper's Magazine, April 2016.
- Mudambi, R. (2008). Location, control and innovation in knowledge-intensive industries. Journal of Economic Geography, 8(5), 699-725.
- Nutt, D. J., King, L. A., & Phillips, L. D. (2010). Drug harms in the UK: A multicriteria decision analysis. The Lancet, 376(9752), 1558-1565.
- Quinones, S. (2015). Dreamland: The true tale of America's opiate epidemic. Bloomsbury Press.
- World Health Organization. (2024). Global status report on alcohol and health. WHO Press.

text_content:

module_title: Episode 21: The Full Spectrum — Who Draws the Line?
title: Knowledge Check: The Full Spectrum
text_content:
This is the final knowledge check of Better Vice Club. It pulls from all four lenses across the whole series: the prohibition map and the harm ranking, the social control pattern and the eight patterns, the full cost and the smile curve, and the literature of consciousness.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

A reminder before you begin: this whole series is a history and policy class, not a how-to, and not an endorsement of any substance. Some of what we studied is deadly, and we tried to be honest about that throughout. If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, the SAMHSA helpline is free, confidential, and open 24 hours at 1-800-662-4357.

When you finish, take the capstone Project, where you apply the full BVC framework, where, who, what pattern, what now, to a commodity of your own choosing. That is the real final exam, and the real point of the whole series.
