(2010) harm ranking, what is the most harmful substance, and is it legal?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"Alcohol, scored most harmful, and it is legal almost everywhere"},{"id":"q5b","text":"Psilocybin, and it is legal"},{"id":"q5c","text":"Cannabis, and it is banned"},{"id":"q5d","text":"Caffeine, and it is legal"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"The peer-reviewed Lancet study scored alcohol the single most harmful substance, yet it is legal almost everywhere, the clearest proof that law does not track harm.","citation":"Lesson: Power, Not Pharmacology"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"Where do psilocybin and LSD fall on the Nutt harm ranking, and what is their legal status?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"Among the least harmful substances, yet both are Schedule I"},{"id":"q6b","text":"Among the most harmful, and both are legal"},{"id":"q6c","text":"In the middle, and both are over-the-counter"},{"id":"q6d","text":"They were not studied"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"Psilocybin and LSD rank among the least harmful substances measured, yet both sit in Schedule I, the most restrictive US category, the same as heroin.","citation":"Lesson: Power, Not Pharmacology"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"What is the core observation when you compare the Nutt ranking to legal status?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"The two most harmful substances are legal, while six of the eight least harmful are Schedule I"},{"id":"q7b","text":"The ranking and the law match perfectly"},{"id":"q7c","text":"All harmful substances are banned"},{"id":"q7d","text":"No substance is legal"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"If law tracked pharmacology, harm and legal status would align. Instead the most harmful are legal and most of the least harmful are Schedule I, the proof of the series thesis.","citation":"Lesson: Power, Not Pharmacology"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"What does the series say substance control has repeatedly been, underneath?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"Population control, aimed at communities those in power saw as a threat"},{"id":"q8b","text":"A purely medical effort to protect health"},{"id":"q8c","text":"A way to raise farm incomes"},{"id":"q8d","text":"An accident of history with no pattern"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"From suppressed coffeehouses to Prohibition to the war on drugs, controlling a substance repeatedly served to control a population perceived as threatening; the substance was not the real concern.","citation":"Lesson: The Social Control Pattern"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"Why were coffeehouses historically suppressed?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"As gathering places for political dissent, as in Charles II's 1675 proclamation and Ottoman bans"},{"id":"q9b","text":"Because coffee was poisonous"},{"id":"q9c","text":"Because no one drank coffee"},{"id":"q9d","text":"Because coffee was too cheap"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"Coffeehouses were suppressed not for the drink but as venues for political organization and dissent, an early example of controlling a space and a population, not a substance.","citation":"Lesson: The Social Control Pattern"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"What did the Ehrlichman admission, reported by Dan Baum, reveal about cannabis criminalization?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"That drug policy was used to target Black communities and the antiwar movement"},{"id":"q10b","text":"That cannabis was found to be extremely dangerous"},{"id":"q10c","text":"That doctors requested the ban"},{"id":"q10d","text":"That it was an accident"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"Baum reported Ehrlichman's documented admission that the war on drugs was used to target Black communities and the antiwar left, evidence that the policy was about population control.","citation":"Lesson: The Social Control Pattern"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What does Pattern 5, 'Power Draws the Line,' state?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"Legal status is set by political and economic power, not pharmacology"},{"id":"q11b","text":"The most powerful chemicals are always banned"},{"id":"q11c","text":"Power has no role in drug law"},{"id":"q11d","text":"Only science draws the line"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"Pattern 5, documented across Season 3, states that political and economic power sets legal status, with the Nutt harm ranking versus legal status as the evidence.","citation":"Lesson: The Eight Patterns"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"What does Pattern 7, 'Extraction Follows Extraction,' describe?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"The same communities bear the costs across centuries, from colonial to corporate to pharmaceutical and criminal extraction"},{"id":"q12b","text":"Mining always comes before farming"},{"id":"q12c","text":"Extraction only happened in Season 1"},{"id":"q12d","text":"Costs are shared evenly by everyone"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"Pattern 7 traces how the same communities repeatedly bear the costs, from colonial commodity extraction to corporate extraction to pharmaceutical and criminal extraction, different centuries, same structure.","citation":"Lesson: The Eight Patterns"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"What does Pattern 8, 'Criminalization Transfers Profit,' state?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"Banning a substance hands its market to criminal organizations"},{"id":"q13b","text":"Banning a substance removes all the money from it"},{"id":"q13c","text":"Criminalization lowers prices"},{"id":"q13d","text":"Criminalization helps farmers most"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Pattern 8 states that criminalization removes legal supply, creates a price premium, and transfers that premium to criminal organizations, as seen from Prohibition to cannabis to cocaine.","citation":"Lesson: The Eight Patterns"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"What is structural racism, as distinct from intentional racism?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"A system that reliably produces racial harm even without every actor intending it"},{"id":"q14b","text":"Only racism that someone openly admits"},{"id":"q14c","text":"A type of building code"},{"id":"q14d","text":"Racism that has no real effects"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"Drawing on Alexander's The New Jim Crow, structural racism describes a system whose structure, who gets policed, charged, and sentenced, channels racial harm even without explicit intent.","citation":"Lesson: Structural, Not Just Intentional"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"Why is the question 'was a policy meant to be racist?' described as a smaller question than another?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"Because the bigger question is whether it reliably produces racial harm, which the series evidence answers"},{"id":"q15b","text":"Because intent is the only thing that matters"},{"id":"q15c","text":"Because policies are never racist"},{"id":"q15d","text":"Because outcomes do not matter"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Asking about intent is narrower than asking whether a policy reliably produces racial harm. The series evidence across three seasons answers that broader, structural question.","citation":"Lesson: Structural, Not Just Intentional"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"How does the alcohol-versus-khat comparison illuminate the relationship between power and policy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"Alcohol causes about 3 million deaths a year and is legal, while khat causes roughly zero and was banned"},{"id":"q16b","text":"Both cause the same number of deaths"},{"id":"q16c","text":"Khat is far deadlier than alcohol"},{"id":"q16d","text":"Neither is regulated"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"Alcohol, legal, causes about 3 million deaths a year, while khat, banned in the UK and US, causes roughly zero, showing policy following economic and political power rather than harm.","citation":"Lesson: The Full Cost"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"About how many deaths does tobacco cause each year, and what is its legal status?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"About 8 million a year, and it is legal everywhere"},{"id":"q17b","text":"About 100 a year, and it is banned"},{"id":"q17c","text":"Zero, and it is legal"},{"id":"q17d","text":"About 8 million, and it is Schedule I"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Tobacco causes about 8 million deaths a year worldwide, yet it is legal everywhere, while far less harmful substances are criminalized.","citation":"Lesson: The Full Cost"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"What does the smile curve (Mudambi, 2008) describe?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"Value capture is highest at the supply chain's ends (design, branding, retail) and lowest in the physical middle (farming, production)"},{"id":"q18b","text":"Everyone in the supply chain earns the same"},{"id":"q18c","text":"Farmers capture the most value"},{"id":"q18d","text":"Value is highest in the middle"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"The smile curve shows value concentrating at the two ends, design and branding on one side, marketing and retail on the other, while the physical labor in the middle captures the least.","citation":"Lesson: The Smile Curve, Confirmed"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"How does the smile curve apply to criminalized markets?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"It still holds, but criminal organizations replace multinational corporations at the profitable ends"},{"id":"q19b","text":"It disappears entirely"},{"id":"q19c","text":"Farmers suddenly capture the most"},{"id":"q19d","text":"It flips upside down"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"In criminalized markets the curve holds, but cartels and criminal networks take the profitable ends, while the coca farmer or poppy harvester still captures the smallest share.","citation":"Lesson: The Smile Curve, Confirmed"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"Which substance is the one exception to the smile curve across all 21 episodes, and why?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"Khat, because its 48-hour freshness requirement blocks multinational consolidation and keeps the supply chain short"},{"id":"q20b","text":"Coffee, because farmers own the brands"},{"id":"q20c","text":"Tobacco, because it is heavily taxed"},{"id":"q20d","text":"There are no exceptions"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"Khat is the lone exception: its 48-hour shelf life prevents corporate consolidation, keeping the chain short and the producer share higher, an example of geography as economic protection.","citation":"Lesson: The Smile Curve, Confirmed"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What happens to a substance's money when it is criminalized?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"The money is not removed; it moves from legal, taxed, regulated hands into criminal ones"},{"id":"q21b","text":"The money disappears completely"},{"id":"q21c","text":"The money goes to the government"},{"id":"q21d","text":"The money goes to the farmers"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"Criminalization removes legal supply and creates a price premium that transfers to whoever controls the illegal market, so the money moves into criminal hands rather than vanishing.","citation":"Lesson: Criminalization Transfers Profit"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What are two examples of criminalization transferring profit to illegal markets?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"Prohibition fueling organized crime, and cocaine's 100-billion-dollar-plus market captured by cartels"},{"id":"q22b","text":"Coffee and tea black markets"},{"id":"q22c","text":"Sugar smuggling rings"},{"id":"q22d","text":"There are no examples"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Alcohol Prohibition gave rise to organized crime, and cocaine's market of more than 100 billion dollars was captured by cartels precisely because criminalization made legal supply impossible.","citation":"Lesson: Criminalization Transfers Profit"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What three kinds of writing did the three seasons produce, according to the ELA synthesis?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"Sacred texts and origins, the texts of civilization, and the texts of power"},{"id":"q23b","text":"Only advertising in all three seasons"},{"id":"q23c","text":"Only scientific papers"},{"id":"q23d","text":"Nothing was written"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Season 1 produced sacred texts and origin stories, Season 2 the texts of civilization, and Season 3 the texts of power, three eras of writing about consciousness.","citation":"Lesson: The Literature of Consciousness"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"What is Pattern A in the literature, 'sacred texts become commodities'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"Sacred writing gets absorbed into commercial narrative, as with Aztec cacao codices and Maria Sabina's chants published without consent"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Sacred texts are always destroyed"},{"id":"q24c","text":"Commercials become sacred"},{"id":"q24d","text":"Nothing is commodified"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Pattern A describes sacred texts being absorbed into commercial narrative, from destroyed Aztec cacao codices replaced by heritage marketing to Maria Sabina's chants published without her community's consent.","citation":"Lesson: Four Patterns in the Literature"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"What is Pattern B, 'propaganda fills the evidence gap'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"Where policy lacked pharmacological justification, propaganda appeared, as in Reefer Madness and 'Just Say No'"},{"id":"q25b","text":"Propaganda only appears when evidence is strong"},{"id":"q25c","text":"Evidence always precedes propaganda"},{"id":"q25d","text":"There was never any propaganda"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Pattern B notes that wherever the evidence for a policy was missing, propaganda filled the gap, from Reefer Madness to cigarette ads to the slogan-based Just Say No campaign.","citation":"Lesson: Four Patterns in the Literature"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"What is Pattern D, 'oral traditions are the most durable archive'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"Community memory preserves what institutions erase, as with a distiller's story kept 130 years and Somali gabay in the diaspora"},{"id":"q26b","text":"Written records always survive best"},{"id":"q26c","text":"Oral traditions disappear fastest"},{"id":"q26d","text":"Archives only exist in books"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Pattern D shows oral traditions outlasting institutional erasure, from a whiskey distiller's family story preserved 130 years to Mazatec ceremony knowledge to Somali gabay surviving in the khat session.","citation":"Lesson: Four Patterns in the Literature"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"What did investigative journalism do in Pattern C?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"Recovered suppressed narratives, as Quinones, Keefe, and Baum did, but only when journalists asked the right questions"},{"id":"q27b","text":"Invented stories from nothing"},{"id":"q27c","text":"Erased the historical record"},{"id":"q27d","text":"Refused to investigate"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"Pattern C describes journalism recovering erased narratives: Quinones on the opioid crisis, Keefe on the Sacklers, and Baum surfacing the Ehrlichman admission, when the right questions are asked.","citation":"Lesson: Four Patterns in the Literature"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"What is the difference between a colonial commodity being legal and a sacred plant being criminal, according to the map?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"It reflects colonial history and power, not the substances' relative harm"},{"id":"q28b","text":"The colonial commodities are simply safer"},{"id":"q28c","text":"The sacred plants are all deadly"},{"id":"q28d","text":"It is purely random"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"The legal split reflects which substances built colonial economic infrastructure and which were tied to marginalized communities, not their measured harm.","citation":"Lesson: The Prohibition Map"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"Which of these are the four patterns the series found in Seasons 1 and 2?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"Geography Insists, It Begins Sacred, Labor Captures the Smallest Share, and Narrative Control"},{"id":"q29b","text":"Only patterns about money"},{"id":"q29c","text":"There were no patterns before Season 3"},{"id":"q29d","text":"Patterns about weather only"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"The first four BVC patterns are Geography Insists, It Begins Sacred, Labor Captures the Smallest Share (the smile curve), and Narrative Control, later joined by four more in Season 3.","citation":"Lesson: The Eight Patterns"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"What does De Quincey's Confessions of an English Opium-Eater represent in the literary inventory?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"The first great addiction narrative, a Season 3 text of power"},{"id":"q30b","text":"A coffee origin myth"},{"id":"q30c","text":"A beer recipe"},{"id":"q30d","text":"A cigarette advertisement"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"De Quincey's Confessions is named as the first great addiction narrative, one of Season 3's texts of power that hold pleasure and destruction at once.","citation":"Lesson: The Literature of Consciousness"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"What is the overall conclusion the series reaches in its finale?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"Across 21 episodes, power and history, not pharmacology, explain which substances are legal and which are criminal"},{"id":"q31b","text":"Every drug law is based purely on science"},{"id":"q31c","text":"There is no pattern across the episodes"},{"id":"q31d","text":"Harm fully explains the law"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Holding all 21 episodes together, the finale concludes that political and economic power and colonial history, not pharmacological evidence, explain the legal map.","citation":"Lesson: The Full Spectrum (intro)"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"How big is the legal alcohol market, roughly, and how does it compare to its harm?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"About 1.5 trillion dollars, and it causes about 3 million deaths a year"},{"id":"q32b","text":"About 100 dollars, and it causes no deaths"},{"id":"q32c","text":"It is illegal and earns nothing"},{"id":"q32d","text":"About 1.5 trillion dollars, and it causes zero deaths"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Alcohol is roughly a 1.5 trillion dollar market that causes about 3 million deaths a year, yet remains legal, while far less harmful substances are banned.","citation":"Lesson: The Full Cost"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"What does the propaganda film Reefer Madness illustrate?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"A fear appeal substituting for evidence where cannabis criminalization had no pharmacological justification"},{"id":"q33b","text":"A careful scientific study"},{"id":"q33c","text":"A coffee advertisement"},{"id":"q33d","text":"An Indigenous origin myth"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Reefer Madness is an example of propaganda filling the evidence gap, using fear in place of evidence to justify cannabis criminalization.","citation":"Lesson: Four Patterns in the Literature"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"Why is this finale described as 'new understanding, not new information'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"It adds no new facts; it shows what all 21 episodes mean when held together"},{"id":"q34b","text":"It introduces a brand-new substance"},{"id":"q34c","text":"It contradicts every earlier episode"},{"id":"q34d","text":"It contains no analysis"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"The finale presents no new substance or facts; its value is synthesis, showing the pattern that emerges only when all 21 episodes are viewed together.","citation":"Lesson: The Full Spectrum (intro)"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"Is the series thesis presented as an opinion or as a conclusion?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"As a research conclusion the evidence across 21 episodes keeps pointing to, not an editorial opinion"},{"id":"q35b","text":"As a personal opinion with no evidence"},{"id":"q35c","text":"As a guess"},{"id":"q35d","text":"As advertising"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"The finale frames its thesis as a documented research conclusion supported by evidence across three seasons, including the peer-reviewed Nutt ranking, not as policy advocacy.","citation":"Lesson: The Full Spectrum (intro)"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, what resource does the series give?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"The SAMHSA helpline, free and confidential 24/7 at 1-800-662-4357"},{"id":"q36b","text":"No resources exist"},{"id":"q36c","text":"Only a podcast"},{"id":"q36d","text":"A marketing hotline"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"Throughout the series, and especially in Season 3, the SAMHSA helpline, 1-800-662-4357, is offered as free, confidential, 24-hour help, because these substances carry real risks.","citation":"Lesson: The Full Spectrum (intro)"}]}
